context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on this city case study rn, can u just explain the whole geographic/demographic data thing from the start?
exchanges.tutor: Let's figure out where you're stuck! Have you picked a city yet? And have you looked at the data table you need to fill out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing confusion but hasn't shared what they've already done or thought about. Before scaffolding anything, I should find out where they actually are in the process - what city they've chosen, what they've gathered so far. This avoids over-explaining things they may already understand and keeps the student active in the process rather than passive recipients of explanation.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but tbh i'm still confused about all of it. i picked a city i think, but can u just walk thru the whole assignment first?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to walk through the whole thing upfront - that's what we'll do together as you work through it! So let's start simple: what city did you pick?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk through the whole assignment, which would be over-scaffolding and doing the work for them. I should redirect them to start with what they have - their city choice - and work from there. A broad explanation of the whole assignment isn't necessary; the assignment description is fairly clear. I should push them to commit to their city choice and start working from there.

exchanges.student: uh maybe Jakarta, but now i'm not even sure if that was a good choice lol. i'm confused what info actually counts for the table.
exchanges.tutor: Jakarta is a solid choice - no need to second-guess it. Now, have you read through the assignment description? It actually lists the data categories you need. What ones can you identify from it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a city but is second-guessing their choice and also expressing confusion about what data to collect. I should address the city choice briefly (Jakarta is a perfectly valid choice) but not dwell on it - the more important thing is to get them moving on the actual task. I'll affirm the city and then redirect to the table itself, asking them what categories they've already identified from the assignment description rather than listing them myself.

exchanges.student: i saw some of them but ngl it all blurred together. i remember population maybe, but can u list what i'm supposed to find?
exchanges.tutor: I won't list them for you - they're right there in the assignment description! Go back and read it carefully one more time. What categories can you pull out this time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to list the categories for them, which would be doing the work for them. The assignment description clearly lists the categories, so I should direct them back to re-read it carefully rather than spoon-feeding the list. I'll be direct but not harsh about this.

exchanges.student: ok wait i think location, population, area maybe? then i get lost again fr. is it like just basic stats or also climate stuff too?
exchanges.tutor: Good, you got three! You're not far off from the full list - there are just a few more. Go back to the assignment description and look carefully. What else do you see mentioned alongside those three?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified three categories correctly and is now asking a good question about scope. Rather than answering directly, I should affirm what they found and nudge them to go back to the assignment description to find the remaining categories themselves. They're close - just need one more careful read.

exchanges.student: maybe income? and something about gdp? tbh the race/ethnicity part and koppen thing are what's messing me up now.
exchanges.tutor: Great - you've got most of them now! Let's tackle the Koppen thing since that seems most unfamiliar. Have you ever heard of climate classification systems before? What do you think a 'climate zone' might tell you about a city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified almost all the required categories, which is good progress. They're stuck on two specific ones: race/ethnicity breakdown and Koppen climate zone. Rather than explaining both, I should pick the one that seems more unfamiliar (Koppen) and ask them what they already know or can find out about it, keeping them active in the discovery process.

exchanges.student: not really tbh, that's why i'm stuck. can u just explain what koppen climate zone means in simple words?
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way - what do you already know about Jakarta's weather or climate in general? And have you tried just googling 'Koppen climate zone Jakarta'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain Koppen directly. I should avoid giving a full explanation, but since this is a factual/definitional concept they genuinely don't know and it's blocking their progress, I can give a minimal nudge in the right direction by asking a guiding question rather than explaining it outright. I'll point them toward looking it up themselves.

exchanges.student: i mean hot and rainy i guess, but idk how that turns into a code. i haven't googled it yet lol.
exchanges.tutor: You're already on the right track with 'hot and rainy' - that intuition will actually help you recognize the right answer when you find it. Go ahead and google it, then come back and tell me what you found!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student hasn't tried googling yet, which is the most obvious first step. I should push them to do that before I offer any more guidance. They already have useful intuition about Jakarta's climate. I'll affirm that and redirect them to actually look it up.

exchanges.student: ok but i'm still confused what the code actually means, like if i see letters i won't know how to use them. can u explain that part rq?
exchanges.tutor: Look it up first - you might find the explanation right there alongside the code. Come back with what you find and we'll make sense of it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pre-emptively asking for an explanation before even looking it up. This is avoidance behavior. I should push them to do the search first - they may find that the resources they encounter explain the codes clearly enough. I shouldn't over-scaffold before they've even tried.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, but the race/ethnicity part is confusing too bc idk if cities even have that data. how am i supposed to know what's valid?
exchanges.tutor: Let's not get ahead of ourselves - you haven't looked up the Koppen zone yet! Finish that first, then we'll tackle race/ethnicity. One thing at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is jumping to a new concern before completing the previous task (looking up Koppen). This is a pattern of avoidance. I should gently point that out and redirect them to finish one thing at a time. Their question about race/ethnicity data is valid but premature right now.
